description.title: Methodology
description.subtitle: How LexEval builds source-grounded rubrics and compares models
description.intro: LexEval evaluates legal-answer quality by separating source-grounded benchmark construction from downstream model comparison.
description.twoLayers: The current implementation combines three connected layers:
description.layerFrankDesc: Source intake, structured extraction, six-pack routing, gold answer generation, locked controller-card creation, human review, and optional variation selection.
description.layerCoreDesc: Setup responses, fixed-k conditioning centroids, response-conditioned proposal, recursive decompose-filter refinement, final weighting, and optional variation-rubric generation.
description.layerDownstreamDesc: Comparison responses, adaptive clustering, centroid scoring, overlays, citation verification, judge panels, dual-track analysis, and final ranking.
description.pipelineNote: Clustering and scoring happen only after the rubric is frozen. Internal setup and judge models remain separated from the user-facing comparison pool.

authors.title: Project Authors
authors.note: Authors are listed alphabetically by last name.
authors.name: Name

modelRegistry.title: Implemented Model Registry
modelRegistry.p1: These are the model roles implemented by the application. Internal setup, reference, and judge models are kept separate from the user-facing comparison pool.
modelRegistry.p2: The same internal model family powers setup responses, refinement, and optional judge panels, while the comparison pool is loaded live from the backend allowlist.
modelRegistry.comparisonNote: The comparison pool is loaded from /api/v1/evaluations/models so this page reflects the implemented backend allowlist.
modelRegistry.error: Could not load the comparison model pool.
modelRegistry.name: Name

stage1.title: Stage 1 -- Legal Case Ingestion
stage1.p1: The user uploads a legal case PDF. The backend extracts full text with pdfplumber and stores it together with the user's legal question.
stage1.p2: If no case text is provided, the system skips Frank and runs the question-only RRD path.

stage2.title: Stage 2 -- Frank Grounding, Review, and Variation Gate
stage2.p1: When source text is available, Frank runs before RRD. It screens the source, extracts doctrine, routes to a B-series pack, and generates the benchmark materials used downstream.
stage2.p2: The pipeline pauses for human review before rubric construction continues. Approval can advance directly to the base question or into the variation workflow.
stage2.screeningDesc: Frank records a 17-item intake screen, an 18-key source extraction, canonical source case name and citation fields, and a question-analysis check.
stage2.routingDesc: The extracted source is routed to one of six packs: marriage, suretyship, one-year, land, UCC 2-201, or executor. Frank then builds a gold packet mapping and predicted failure modes.
stage2.controllerDesc: Frank generates a weak reference answer, a clean benchmark gold answer, and a locked 36-field controller card that fixes pack identity, base-question metadata, and citation-verification mode.
stage2.reviewDesc: Reviewers can approve, reject, or reroute. Approved rubrics move to `variation_pending`, where the reviewer can skip variation, request a variation menu, or select a lane-coded variation for dual-rubric mode.

stage3.title: Stage 3 -- RRD Setup Response Generation
stage3.p1: After Frank review completes, or immediately on the question-only path, four internal setup models generate 25 responses each for a total of 100 setup responses.
stage3.p2: The Frank gold answer becomes the strong reference on the Frank path. On the question-only path, the strong and weak reference pair comes from the internal setup roster.
stage3.modelNote: These models are excluded from the comparison pool so they cannot influence a rubric they will later be evaluated against.

stage4.title: Stage 4 -- Fixed-k Conditioning Sample
stage4.p1: The 100 setup responses are embedded with all-mpnet-base-v2 and clustered with fixed k = 8.
stage4.p2: The response with highest cosine similarity to each cluster centroid becomes a conditioning example for the initial rubric proposal.
stage4.deviation: LexEval deviates from the RRD paper here: instead of sampling 8 responses randomly, it clusters 100 setup responses and uses 8 representative centroids.

stage5.title: Stage 5 -- Response-Conditioned Initial Rubric Proposal
stage5.p1: The control model proposes an initial rubric conditioned on the legal question, the 8 centroid responses, and doctrine-pack context when available.
stage5.p2: Each criterion is normalized into the implemented schema with id, name, description, weight, and module_id when module structure is present.

stage6.title: Stage 6 -- Recursive Decompose-Filter Loop
stage6.p1: This is the core RRD refinement stage. Broad criteria are decomposed, then filtered for misalignment and redundancy until the loop converges or hits a stopping condition.
stage6.decompositionDesc: A criterion is considered too broad when it passes more than 2 of the 8 centroid texts. It is then decomposed into narrower child criteria, with only one decomposition depth allowed.
stage6.misalignmentDesc: A child criterion is rejected only when it passes the weak reference and fails the strong reference. On the Frank path, the strong reference is the approved Frank gold answer.
stage6.redundancyDesc: Each surviving child criterion is checked against already accepted criteria and removed if the control model judges it redundant.
stage6.stoppingDesc: The implemented loop stops when rejected proposals reach 5, when no broad criteria remain, or when the hard cap of 20 passes is reached.

stage7.title: Stage 7 -- Final Weighting, Karthic Enrichment, and Variation Rubric
stage7.p1: After refinement, LexEval computes whitened-uniform criterion weights and blends module priors when module structure is present.
stage7.p2: The rubric can then be enriched with Karthic row-card fields. If a reviewer selected a lane and enabled dual-rubric mode, the system can also generate a separate variation rubric.
stage7.karthicDesc: Row-card enrichment adds fields such as row codes, NA guidance, golden-target summaries, omissions, contradiction flags, scoring anchors, and primary failure labels, followed by an overlap audit.
stage7.variationDesc: Approved rubrics can persist selected_lane_code, variation_question, and variation_criteria. The base rubric remains in criteria, while the variation rubric is stored separately.

stage8.title: Stage 8 -- Comparison Response Generation
stage8.p1: The user selects 2 to 5 models from the comparison pool. Each selected model generates 40 base responses, and dual-rubric runs also generate 40 variation responses per model.
stage8.p2: Each stored response records question_version as either base or variation, allowing the analysis stage to separate the two tracks.
stage8.exclusionDesc: A model is excluded from the comparison pool if it participates in setup, rubric construction, or judging.

stage9.title: Stage 9 -- Dasha Analysis: Clustering, Scoring, and Panels
stage9.p1: Base responses are embedded and clustered with adaptive silhouette-score selection. Centroids are then scored against the frozen rubric and stored with heuristic, uniform, and whitened-uniform views.
stage9.p2: For Frank-enhanced rubrics, the analysis layer also produces failure tags, metadata tags, centroid composition, Dasha overlays, citation verification, and optional judge-panel outputs.
stage9.heuristic: Heuristic: RRD-assigned or blended weights from the frozen rubric
stage9.whitened: Whitened Uniform: normalized comparison view across clusters
stage9.tagsDesc: The analysis layer can add Module 0 metadata, failure tags, centroid composition blocks, penalties, caps, final scores, and case-citation verification metadata.
stage9.dualTrackDesc: When dual_rubric_mode is active, base and variation responses are loaded separately by question_version. If variation responses are missing, the system falls back to rescoring base centroids against the variation rubric.
stage9.judgePanelDesc: Optional internal judge models can vote on centroid rankings. When no strict majority is reached, the system records judge votes and raises a zak_review_flag.

stage10.title: Stage 10 -- Ranking
stage10.p1: The primary ranking rule remains unchanged: identify the winning cluster under the heuristic track and rank models by their share inside that cluster.
stage10.p2: Dasha final scores, penalties, caps, and dual-track outputs are reported in parallel, but they do not replace the winning-cluster ranking rule.

controlModel.title: Control Model
controlModel.p1: deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3 serves as the control model for proposal, refinement, centroid scoring, and the default single-judge path when a judge panel is not supplied.

variation.title: Variation Selection
variation.description: Optionally generate a variation of the base question before building the rubric.

judgePanel.title: Judge Panel
judgePanel.description: Select 1–3 internal models as judges. Comparison pool models are not eligible.

zak.title: Zak Review Required
zak.description: No judge panel majority was reached. Disputed clusters:

reviewGate.title: Review Required
reviewGate.description: The FrankInstructions pipeline requires human approval before continuing.
reviewGate.rejected: This rubric was rejected during review.
